data.title: Reddit: A Good Time To Buy The Sharp Dip (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
When markets are this volatile, it's the exact perfect time to be an active investor. I continue to individually assess every stock in my portfolio, as valuations are gyrating wildly and each company's impact from tariffs and the slowing macroeconomy will be different.

Reddit (NYSE:RDDT), in particular, is a stock that I think has tremendous rebound potential. The fast-growing social media platform, best known for its candid discussion forums, has seen its share price collapse ~40% since the start of January. For much of 2024, Reddit was trading at unbuyable levels from a valuation perspective: for investors who have held out patiently on the sidelines, this is the right dip to buy.

I was initially quite bullish on Reddish immediately after its IPO, but my outlook on the stock soured when its valuation multiples started climbing. I downgraded Reddit to a sell rating earlier this year in January, when the stock was just below $200 per share. Today, however, Reddit is hovering around the $100 mark, down ~50% from its peaks, while its user base continues to showcase extraordinary growth that no other social media platform can match. As such, I'm giving Reddit a two-notch upgrade to a buy rating.

Since valuation is the major tiebreaker in my decision to reinvest in Reddit, and it's the main thing that has changed in the Reddit story over the past few months, let's start with a discussion of Reddit's latest multiples. At current share prices near $100, Reddit trades at a market cap of $18.30 billion. After we net off the $1.84 billion of cash on Reddit's latest balance sheet, the company's resulting enterprise value is $16.46 billion.

Reddit does not provide full-year guidance, but for Q1, the company is pointing to $360-$370 million in revenue, or 48-52% y/y growth; and $80-$90 million in adjusted EBITDA (a 23.3% midpoint margin that implies nearly 20 points of y/y margin expansion).

Meanwhile, Wall Street analysts have a consensus estimate of $1.74 billion in revenue for this year, or 37% y/y growth. This puts Reddit at a 9.4x EV/FY25 revenue multiple.

Yes, it's true that Reddit trades at a richer valuation multiple than the industry bellwether, Meta (META). But at the same time, Meta is seeing far more sluggish user growth trends, and the Street is only calling for low-teens growth for Meta in 2025 while Reddit is growing nearly 3x as quickly. I'd gladly pay a two-turn premium for Reddit now (and note that before the recent stock market crash that converged Reddit and Meta's multiples more closely, Reddit traded at more than double Meta's valuation, hence my prior skittishness on the stock).

The long-term bull case for Reddit

This reasonable valuation buys investors a strong list of long-term tailwinds. In my view, the top reasons to be bullish on Reddit are:

  • Far superior growth rates versus social media peers proves the differentiation of its brand. As just mentioned, Reddit is expected to grow at a ~3x pace versus Meta. In my view, the company has a wide moat versus other social media peers: Reddit focuses less on pictures, videos and personal promotion; and rather on discussion boards and topics of interest.
  • Unlocking international growth via machine translation into eight foreign languages. Reddit is deploying automatic machine translation to serve up English-language content to more Reddit users across the globe, currently available in eight languages. International language content is searchable on Google, and the faster the company is able to translate its content into other languages, the more organic traffic it can bring to its sites.
  • Diversifying monetization streams, especially with data licensing opportunity. Like most social media companies, Reddit generates the majority of its revenue from selling advertising space. But like X, the company has also started selling a data licensing feed as well as setting up a user e-commerce ecosystem to drive more revenue. The company cited a third-party study that noted that 40% of internet users seek advice and reviews on Reddit before purchasing a product: which makes Reddit discussion boards incredibly valuable data for marketers.
  • Profitable bones. Reddit is scaling tremendously, and it is already minting adjusted EBITDA in a 20-30% range. To me, the fact that revenue is growing north of 30% y/y and adjusted EBITDA is growing substantially faster is yet another reason why Reddit's relatively premium near-term valuation multiples are justifiable.

The risk investors have their eyes on: a hiccup in U.S. user growth?

With all of these positives being said, however, there is a reason Reddit stock has faltered this year beyond just the broader market turmoil, and investors should go into this stock with eyes wide open.

For social media companies, the core long-term driver for growth is user growth: but especially growth in the U.S., because (at least for now) U.S. users generate substantially more advertising revenue than users in other regions.

Reddit has never seen a sequential decline in users until Q4, when its U.S. user base dipped slightly to 48.0 million DAUs (daily active users).

The company cited a change in the Google search algorithm as the core reason why. On the Q4 earnings call, CEO Steve Huffman explained the situation as follows:

Late in Q4, we did experience some volatility from Google Search triggered by a periodic algorithm change but traffic from search has recovered so far in Q1 and we've regained momentum. What happened wasn't unusual, referrals from search fluctuate from time to time and they primarily affect logged-out users. Our teams have navigated many algorithm updates over the years and did an excellent job adapting to these latest changes effectively. This particular swing was interesting because, along with it, we saw a corresponding increase in the query term, Reddit which suggests users are searching with the specific intent of reaching Reddit and this propensity continues to rise.

We typically see two types of users on Reddit: those who scroll and those who seek. Scrollers engage with Reddit for its core community and conversation product, while seekers come to, or end up on, Reddit for answers to their questions. Just a few years ago, adding Reddit to the end of your search query felt novel. Today, it's a common way for people to find trusted information, recommendations and advice. Regardless of why or where a user starts their journey, they should have a seamless path to discovering what they need on Reddit."

The good news is that management says the company has regained momentum in Q1, so we hope to see Reddit return to sequential U.S. growth. Meanwhile, overall DAUs are still climbing, thanks to 4.7 million sequential adds in international markets - which accelerated from 4.3 million adds in Q3, and which we expect to continue picking up steam as machine translation continues to unlock more local-language content.

Another helpful offset to weaker U.S. user growth: ARPU continues to climb, both in the U.S. (+28% y/y) and internationally (+25% y/y), feeding Reddit's tremendous 71% y/y revenue growth in Q4 to $427.7 million, which beat Wall Street's expectations of just $405.5 million (+62% y/y).

Reddit's revenue is also climbing much faster than its cost base, leading to substantial margin accretion. As shown in the chart below, Reddit's revenue growth outpaced its cost growth by 3.5x, while adjusted EBITDA margins climbed 27 points y/y to 36.1%:

We have to underscore and emphasize that no other company in the social media space has anything close to these metrics. They also face similar issues, with stagnant or declining U.S. users. Snap (SNAP), for example, has seen flat DAUs in the U.S. for the entirety of FY24, while its revenue grew only double digits in Q4 (compared to Reddit's 72% y/y growth) and its adjusted EBITDA margin of 18% was half of Reddit's.

Q1 look-ahead

The next major catalyst for Reddit will be its Q1 earnings release, which will be announced on May 1.

As previously mentioned, the company is guiding to 48-52% y/y growth on the top line, with a 23.3% adjusted EBITDA margin that implies 19 points of y/y expansion at the midpoint versus just a 4.1% in the year-ago Q1:

Reddit is not a "set it and forget it" type of stock: the volatility that its stock has seen in Q1 is proof of that. There are several key items I'm watching for in Q1 that could change my stance on the stock:

  • U.S. user trends. Of course, this is the big-ticket item we need to assess critically. Management vaguely noted that it "regained momentum" in early Q1 after the company received less traffic inflow from Google search in Q4, but we need to see if the company can return to sequential growth in U.S. DAUs.
  • Is the profit guidance conservative? In the prior-year Q4 to Q1, adjusted EBITDA margins fell from 9.3% in Q4'23 to 4.1% in Q1'24. This year's guide implies a 36.1% margin in Q4 dropping to 23.3% in Q1. The thirteen-point decline seems steep: we should watch for the company's ability to exceed this forecast.

Key takeaways

All in all, however, I believe Reddit's sharp ~50% fall from peaks has rendered the stock incredibly attractive again. To me, Reddit's risks (both the company-specific U.S. user slowdown as well as general tariff-war risks, as a slowing macroeconomy may impact advertiser demand) are already priced into the stock, which is a great buy-the-dip candidate at <10x forward revenue.

In my view, investors will benefit from buying the stock ahead of the May 1 earnings date, where relief on U.S. user trends may help Reddit recover closer to prior heights.

data.title: BDCs Offer The First Good Buying Opportunity After The Tariff Shock
data.description:
Introduction

After Trump announced the new reciprocal tariff system, the market tanked drastically. The S&P 500 had its worst week since March 2020. The Nasdaq dropped 10% this week, entering bear-market territory (down 20% or more from its all-time high). This is due to expectations of a global trade war and a (potentially global) recession following it. Recession fears have sparked before, but went through the roof this week. JPMorgan, for example, raised its odds for a US and global recession to 60%, up from 40%, which has also been upped a few times already.

Disruptive U.S. policies have been recognized as the biggest risk to the global outlook all year," the brokerage said in a note on Thursday, adding that the country's trade policy has turned less business friendly than anticipated.

The effect... is likely to be magnified through (tariff) retaliation, a slide in U.S. business sentiment and supply-chain disruptions."

These recession fears are also indicated by rapidly rising credit spreads. Business Development Companies (BDCs) react negatively to rising credit spreads and have therefore shed a lot of market capitalization in the last few days. In my opinion, this represents one of the first buying opportunities. In the following, I will explain credit spreads, give my opinion on their development, and explain why I think that Ares Capital, Capital Southwest, and the VanEck BDC Income ETF are buys right now.

What Are BDCs, and How Do They Earn Money?

Business Development Companies (BDCs) are publicly traded investment firms that provide debt and equity financing to small and mid-sized private U.S. companies. These businesses usually have a hard time accessing traditional capital markets. Instead, they lend from BDCs. BDCs offer financing to these companies and access to private credits for retail investors.

BDCs earn money primarily through interest payments they receive for the money they lend out. They also receive fees, such as origination fees, prepayment fees, and other lending-related charges. In some cases, they receive equity stakes or warrants in said companies and can realize capital gains.

By law, BDCs have to distribute at least 90% of their taxable income to shareholders. Hence, they are known for having high dividend yields.

Credit Spreads and Their Implications

The credit spread is the difference in yield between a bond and a risk-free rate (like U.S. Treasuries) to compensate for credit/default risk. The option-adjusted spread (OAS) adjusts the credit spread by accounting for other influences, like options work into the bond. It shows solely the credit and liquidity risk, removing the influence of options. The implications of rising credit spreads are as follows:

  • Higher perceived risk of default in the market
  • Borrowing becomes more expensive for companies
  • May signal economic stress or recession fears
  • Investors demand more return for risk

Considering this, it is not surprising that BDCs react negatively and very sensitively to rising credit spreads. They lend money, so higher defaults could cost them dearly. They borrow money themselves, so this has negative implications, which are partly offset by them charging more to their clients. Higher economic stress or recession fears are probably the bigger problem because they could drive insolvencies and/or pressure their clients into delaying installments. This would obviously be very bad for BDCs.

Let me show you the changes in credit spreads and how they compare to the past:

YTD changes in credit spreads for BBB-CCC credits (the kind of credits BDCs work with):

Here are the % rates in which these spreads have increased since the beginning of the year:

Those are massive growth rates for such a short time. But, if we look at the big picture, we see that credit spreads are still well below the highs of 2022, for example.

BDCs' Reaction to Rising Credit Spreads

BDCs react very sensitively to rising credit spreads:

Whenever credit spreads rise sharply (bottom chart), BDCs fall (top chart). Even the ETF, as shown here, reacts drastically to the upward swings in credit spreads.

Right now, you can see a theatrical reaction, which is not only due to rising credit spreads but also due to the market's fears of a global trade war and a subsequent recession.

Credit Spreads Forecast

As I said, credit spreads still have a lot of room to run until the heights of 2022, for example, are reached. But as of now, I expect President Trump to move away from tariffs rather quickly. I will probably write an entire article about that, but in short, my thesis is that Trump wants to pressure Powell into lowering rates. The U.S. has to refinance a substantial amount of its debt in 2025. According to data from the Congressional Budget Office (CBO), on June 30, 2025, roughly $132 billion of securities will mature. Trump has already mentioned that his goal is to refinance at the lowest rates possible, and has therefore told Powell to cut rates several times already.

The only fast way to pressure the Fed into lowering rates is by crashing the stock market. This goal is now accomplished. Tariffs, however, are inflationary and would therefore put the Fed in the position of raising rates-which would be contradictory to what Trump wants. The only way to resolve this is by canceling the tariffs before they take effect and can drive inflation up. I think Trump will use any display of cooperation by other countries to exempt them from the tariffs.

Since tariffs are the only thing right now driving credit spreads, I expect them to decline quickly as soon as the tariff issue is resolved.

Suitable Investments

I like, and own, Ares Capital (NASDAQ:ARCC) and Capital Southwest (NASDAQ:CSWC). I also like, but do not own, the VanEck BDC Income ETF (NYSEARCA:BIZD). Let's have a look at these three as possible investments.

Ares Capital

ARCC has existed since 2004 and has a tremendous track record of good execution and dividend payouts. Here is a quote from Seeking Alpha's stock page:

Ares Capital Corporation is a business development company specializing in acquisition, recapitalization, mezzanine debt, restructurings, rescue financing, and leveraged buyout transactions of middle market companies. It also makes growth capital and general refinancing. It prefers to make investments in companies engaged in the basic and growth manufacturing, business services, consumer products, health care products and services, and information technology service sectors."

I like ARCC in particular because its management has shown good execution and the dividend, the prime argument for an investment in BDCs, has an amazing track record:

Even in the Great Financial Crisis, ARCC only reduced their dividend by 16.7% which is very good considering the severeness the GFC brought with it. As of now, ARCC has a dividend yield of 9.6%, which is the highest since March 2023:

The share price fell below the August 2024 low and is now back on the level from January 2024:

The forward P/E well too and is currently well below its 10-year average:

The share price drop presents an attractive buying opportunity right now.

Capital Southwest

CSWC was founded in 1961 and belongs to the oldest BDCs there is. Since then, they too have done an amazing job and can show a good track-record. Here is a quote from Seeking Alpha's stock page:

Capital Southwest Corporation is a business development company specializing in credit and private equity and venture capital investments in middle market companies, mezzanine, later stage, mature, late venture, emerging growth, buyouts, industry consolidation, recapitalizations and growth capital investments. It does not invest in startups, publicly traded companies, real estate developments, project finance opportunities, oil and gas exploration businesses, troubled companies, turnarounds, and companies in which significant senior management is departing."

I like CSWC because they are a bit more volatile than ARCC and therefore offer more entry points. However, this does also mean that you have to watch the position more closely. Another reason why I like CSWC so much is the MASSIVE dividend of 12.9%.

While the dividend history does not look as clean as ARCC's, CSWC still pays a steady and growing dividend. They also regularly pay special dividends of substantial amount.

Similar to ARCC, the recent drop brought the dividend yield back to levels last seen in 2023.

The share price is back to levels last seen in June 2023 and the range of 2022:

The forward P/E dropped to levels last seen in June 2023 and well below the 10-year average:

The recent share price drop in addition to declines in the recent past, presents an attractive buying opportunity in my opinion.

VanEck BDC Income ETF

If you don't feel good investing in individual BDCs, you can choose the VanEck BDC Income ETF instead.

The ETF was launched in 2013 and has $1.34 billion in total assets. The total expense ratio is anything but cheap at 13.33%. You directly pay 0.42% (management + other expenses), while the rest are costs of the underlying funds, which are baked into the performance of the ETF. The track record regarding its underlying index is very good, with BIZD having only minor differences:

The ETF consisted of 30 holdings at the time with Ares Capital being the largest one with almost 20% weighting.

CSWC is number 14 sorted after weight.

You're currently able to buy the ETF at a discount of -0.26% to the net asset value (NAV).

The share price is almost back to the 2022 and 2023 lows, giving you the chance to buy very low compared to 2024:

This brought the dividend yield up to 12.6%, the highest since October 2020:

The dividend history is very consistent:

If you don't want to put in the work, or don't have the time to do so, to find the best BDCs, the BIZD ETF is a good alternative.

All three have outperformed the S&P 500 in the last five years by a wide margin:

Looking as far back as we can (ARCC's founding in 2004), we see that both CSWC and ARCC beat the S&P 500. CSWC had a spin-off in 2025 which dropped the price. If this hadn't happened, the total return would be higher than that of the S&P 500.

Risk to My Thesis

The number one risk is probably that President Trump does not lower or eliminate tariffs. This would send credit spreads higher and BDCs lower. However, this would send everything lower, not only BDCs. It would also likely mean a recession is coming, which would be a reason for me to hold BDCs for the dividend. I would rather hold dividend stocks than non-dividend stocks in a recession.

The second risk is that the Fed cuts rates. This will affect BDCs, but not as much as you might think. Lower rates give them the ability to borrow more cheaply while charging less to their borrowers. This should translate to a slightly negative effect.

Conclusion

The upswing in credit spreads has put pressure on the share prices of BDCs, with the probability that the share prices will rise again when credit spreads decline-which I think shouldn't take much longer.

The current prices of ARCC, CSWC, and BIZD offer very good dividend yields, and the valuations are attractive. ARCC and CSWC, as well as the ETF, have outperformed the S&P 500, and I see that continuing.

data.title: Ranger Energy Services Shows Signs Of Strength (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Ranger: Steady Despite Adversity

I discussed Ranger Energy Services (NYSE:RNGR) in the past, and you can read the previous article here, published on January 8, 2025. In early 2025, the completion softness continued. However, the company managed to outdo the competition because demand for its super-spec rigs has been strong. The company’s new gas processing business has been a highlight, although the Wireline Services segment remained under pressure.

RNGR has adjusted its service mix in response to market conditions, which helped EBITDA margin remain resilient in recent times. It also has sufficient liquidity, allowing it to increase dividends in early 2025. The stock appears reasonably valued, with a positive bias, compared to its peers. Given the company’s resilience to market challenges, I would ask investors to "Buy" the stock.

Why Do I Upgrade My Rating?

In my previous iteration in January 2025, I rated it a “Hold.” I wrote:

Recently, RNGR has thrived well, outdoing the decline in completion activity. It has shifted to the production-focused business model. The company's high-specification rigs, coil tubing, plug-and-abandonment, and Torrent service lines have led the charge. With significant excess asset deployment capacity, I think Ranger can do even better when the drilling and completion activities recover on US soil… However, its key operating segment will be under pressure due to the decline in typical seasonal activity in Q4.

By the start of 2025, RNGR’s sales of high specification rigs and investment in high-growth areas kept running ahead of many peers. Its adjusted EBITDA margin remained relatively steady in Q4. Hourly rig rates increased, too. The balance sheet became more robust in FY2024. Given the relative valuation multiples, I upgrade it to a “Buy” rating.

Market And Strategy

The US rig count and completion activity have not been encouraging in recent times. But RNGR has strategized a business model that remained resilient during the downturn. It outperformed the energy sector in the past year despite the upstream industry consolidation. The primary reason for its strong performance is the high specification rigs because of its focus on the customers' production base. Increased efficiency resulted in 20% year-over-year EBITDA growth in the past three quarters until Q4.

RNG has also selectively invested in high-margin growth areas. Apart from the rig business, it has invested in ancillary services consisting of smaller businesses. As a result, its adjusted EBITDA margins increased from 18% to over 21% from FY2023 to FY2024. In Torrent, the management expects to see EBITDA double and achieve full utilization in this business in 2025.

In this context, I will highlight the infield gas processing business - Torrent. It provides modular mobile equipment to capture wellhead natural gas. The process has uses in mobile power generation, dual fuel frac, and cryptocurrency mining.

What’s The Outlook?

At the start of 2025, RNGR’s performance was hit by adverse weather. Given the current pressure on the energy market, I expect the US onshore business to remain depressed in 1H 2025. It may recover partially in 2H 2025. In these circumstances, its management expects the adjusted EBITDA to fall below $20 million. In Q4, it recorded an adjusted EBITDA of $22 million. If commodity prices stabilize, 2H 2025 may see a modest uptick.

In the HSR (High Specification Rigs) segment, Ranger sees year-over-year growth. In Wireline, it does not expect to see significant improvement in broader market conditions despite growth in conventional wireline work and RNGR’s production-exposed approach. Revenues may decline, but EBITDA margins can improve to the “high single digits.”

My Estimates

Over the past 11 quarters, RNGR's adjusted EBITDA increased by 22%, on average. Given the current growth drivers, I expect the EBITDA to secure a slightly higher growth trajectory, although its topline growth can stabilize. Over the next four quarters, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase by 22%-25%.

Q4 Segment Results

As disclosed in the press release published on March 3, in Q4 2024, revenues in the High Specification Rigs segment increased by 10% compared to a year ago. Adjusted EBITDA in this segment increased by 23% during this period. Here, hourly rig rates increased in Q4 following the addition of ancillary equipment attached rig packages.

The Wireline Services segment saw a 46% year-over-year revenue fall in Q4, while its adjusted EBITDA declined by 93%. The completed stage counts decreased by 64% in the past year due to lower operational activity. Also, pricing pressure remains in the wireline business. As operating income plummets, the company is adjusting its service mix in response to market conditions.

Revenues in the Processing Solutions and Ancillary Services segment witnessed an 8% rise year-over-year in Q4 2024. The adjusted EBITDA increased by 51%. Increased operational activity in several service lines, including the Torrent business, contributed to the performance improvement.

Dividend Hike And Cash Flows

RNGR's debt-to-equity was 0.08x as of December 31, 2024 – unchanged over the past year. Its liquidity was $112 million as of that date. In the past year, it repurchased shares worth $15.5 million. At the start of 2025, it increased its quarterly dividend by 20%, reflecting the management’s confidence in the stability and strength of the business. It also delivers on its promise to return capital to shareholders.

In FY2024, the company's cash flow from operations (or CFO) decreased marginally by 7% compared to a year ago. As the cash & equivalents balance increased substantially in FY2024, the company’s net debt turned negative, which reflects a robust balance sheet health.

Wall Street Rating

According to Seeking Alpha, one sell-side analyst rated RNGR a "buy" ("strong buy"), while none recommended a "Hold" or a "Sell." The consensus target price is $20, which yields 70% returns at the current price. Based on my estimates, the Wall Street analysts have overly excessive expectations for the stock.

Relative Valuation And Target Price

RNGR's forward EV/EBITDA multiple is expected to contract versus the current EV/EBITDA. Also, the fall is less sharp than its peers, indicating a less steep rise in EBITDA for RNGR than its peers. This typically results in a lower EV/EBITDA multiple. The company's EV/EBITDA multiple (3.6x) is significantly lower than its peers' (NINE, KLXE, and BOOM) average of 6.5x. So, the stock is reasonably valued, with a positive bias, compared to its peers. If it trades at the industry average, the stock price can step up by 77%.

As I discussed earlier in the article, I expect 22%- 25% adjusted EBITDA growth in the next four quarters. Feeding these values into the EV calculation and applying the current sell-side EV/EBITDA multiple of 3.6x, I think the stock should trade between $13.68 and $13.9, implying approximately 17% upside.

What's The Take On RNGR?

At the start of 2025, RNGR has strengthened its focus on high-specification rigs customers' production base, resulting in RNGR’s operating margin expansion in Q4. Recently, it invested in ancillary services and the infield gas processing business, which has uses in mobile power generation, dual fuel frac, and cryptocurrency mining. So, the stock significantly outperformed the VanEck Vectors Oil Services ETF (OIH) in the past year.

However, the Wireline business has remained an area of concern during the recent completion activity downturn. Pricing pressure continues in the US onshore industry. Although cash flows declined in FY2024, RNGR maintained a robust balance sheet. The relative valuation multiples appear balanced, but I think they have a scope of expanding given the company’s strong strategies. This can create opportunities for increased returns for investors. So, I re-rate it a "Buy."

data.title: 'How I Would Invest $1,000,000 In Retirement': A Critical Analysis
data.description:
A friend recently forwarded me a short video by Tyler Gardner, a former financial adviser, who has over a million followers on various social media channels. He had an interesting take on how to generate retirement income if you start with $1 million.

I know what you're thinking: I shouldn't be trolling social media for investing tips. Good point; I agree. But technically, I'm not following the advice, I'm reviewing it.

In this era of clickbait, I couldn't resist finding out what Tyler was serving up in his 96-second video.

Tyler's Approach

According to the video, here's how Tyler would invest $1 million in retirement:

1. "I would invest 100% of my money in a blue-chip, dividend fund that paid out about 3.5% in dividends each year."

2. In early January, sell 4% of my fund to generate cash.

3. Take social security at age 62 for another $25,000 per year of pretax income.

4. Watch the all-stock fund grow at the historical average of 8% per year, even after selling 4% annually. Tyler says, "in 16 years my initial $1 million should be worth closer to $3.49 million."

The Review

Could this really work? Hmm. Income without eroding the corpus: that's an investor's dream.

Let's break this analysis down into several parts:

  • Replication: Can we replicate Tyler's approach with a fund?

  • Performance: How would this approach have fared in real life, over the past 20 years?

  • Alternatives: What are alternatives to this approach?

I'll ignore the part about taking social security at 62, since that does not affect the portfolio or investment income that Tyler describes.

I'll also ignore the detail about the dividend fund paying "3.5% in dividends" since we're more concerned with total return and less concerned with the exact dividend payout percentage. After all, to generate income we could sell shares in a fund with a lovely, stable total return even if that fund doesn't pay a dividend. But we'll still look for blue-chip, dividend funds in order to follow Tyler's recommendation.

Replication: can we replicate the type of fund mentioned?

First up, what "blue-chip, dividend fund" would be good to use for this investing scenario? Let's look for blue-chip and dividend funds at Vanguard, iShares, Fidelity with at least 20 years of history. Here are six that meet the criteria and that we are already tracking.

  • iShares Select Dividend ETF (DVY)

  • Fidelity Blue Chip Growth Fund (FBGRX)

  • Fidelity Dividend Growth Fund (FDGFX)

  • Fidelity Equity Dividend Income Fund (FEQTX)

  • Fidelity Strategic Dividend & Income Fund (FSDIX)

  • Vanguard Dividend Growth (VDIGX)

Let's look at the risk and return metrics for these six funds over the past 20 years. For comparison, we can also add two benchmarks: an S&P 500 fund (SPY) and a Balanced Portfolio consisting of 60% equities and 40% bonds (s.6040).

Exhibit A: Performance and risk for selected Blue Chip and Dividend Funds

Fund name
