We can also look at the risk vs. return data in a scatterplot. Let's use Downside Deviation as the risk measure. We like to use Downside Deviation (instead of Standard Deviation) as the risk metric since Downside Deviation does not penalize for upward swings in performance, unlike Standard Deviation. Exhibit B plots the risk vs. return for the six portfolios plus two benchmarks.

Exhibit B: Risk vs. Return for selected dividend Funds (over 20 years)

According to Tyler, you can invest in a fund, take out 4% annually, and still have 8% annual return even after the 4% withdrawal. This would seem to require a 12% annual return: 4% for the annual withdrawal plus 8% for continued compound growth.

In Tyler's video, he provides specific details on this compounding effect. He says that "in 16 years my initial $1 million should be worth closer to $3.49 million." Let's double-check what interest rate (after withdrawals) would be needed to achieve that.

We can calculate the required annual interest rate. Let r be the interest rate we want to find. So if $1 million is multiplied by (1 + r) for 16 years, then we want to end up with $3.49 million to match Tyler's scenario.

So we have the following equation, which we can solve for r.

$1,000,000 * (1 + r) ^ 16 = $3,490,000

Solving this for r, we get 8.16%, which is close to the "average of 8% per year" that Tyler refers to, so his math checks out.

But which fund with "Dividend" in its name has a total return of around 12% annually? Looking back at Exhibit A, we couldn't find a single one at Fidelity, iShares, or Vanguard.

Should we stop here and mark this as a "BUSTED" myth? Or should we keep the investigation going? Let's continue by choosing a high-performing "blue chip" fund without strictly following Tyler's advice that the fund also be a "dividend fund." In other words, we can delete the "dividend" requirement but keep the "blue chip" requirement.

Based on the set of funds we've selected, that leaves us with Fidelity Blue Chip Growth Fund. Of course, there are many other "blue chip," large-cap funds without the exact words "blue chip" in their names, but let's run with FBGRX.

As we look closer at FBGRX, we see that it's tech-heavy, with a high level of risk. But FBGRX has posted solid returns over the past few years.

Performance: How would this approach have fared in real life?

Next, let's model Tyler's approach using FBGRX as the chosen fund. We'll see how this would have fared over the past 20 years.

In other words, if we started on Jan 1, 2005 with $1 million… what would we end up with on Dec 31, 2024?

Let's look at the following growth of $1 million over the past 20 years using these scenarios:

  • Scenario A. FBGRX total return with no withdrawals, for comparison.

  • Scenario B. FBGRX total return with 4% annual withdrawals. This is the withdrawal amount that Tyler proposes.

  • Scenario C. Vanguard Dividend Growth total return with 4% annual withdrawals. This is the best fund we found with "dividend" in its name, so this is the closest comparison to Tyler's proposed scenario.

Exhibit C: Growth of $1 million over the past 20 years, under three scenarios

With Scenario A, FBGRX with no withdrawals, we have $12.6 million at the end of 20 years. Nice! But this is the scenario without annual distributions, so we need to move on to Scenario B.

With Scenario B, FBGRX with annual 4% withdrawals, we have $5.6 million at the end of 20 years. This appears to confirm Tyler's proposed methodology. But note that this is not a "dividend fund" that Tyler specifies. Also note that had you invested in Scenario B over these past 20 years, you would have needed to:

  • Endure highly variable annual withdrawals. (After Year 5, there was a 35% drop in annual distributions compared to Year 1)

  • Weather significant drawdowns. For example, just over 5 years into this plan (in Month 62) the portfolio value was 43% below where it started.

  • Wait several years for the distributions to increase. After Year 7, the annual withdrawal provided only 2.4% more cash than the first distribution after Year 1. That does not come close to keeping up with inflation.

With Scenario C, we look at the best dividend fund that we found-- the Vanguard Dividend Growth. With annual 4% withdrawals, we end up with $3.3 million. This appears to get close to Tyler's scenario, but there's one more wrinkle: we're looking at the past 20 years, and for some reason Tyler referred to a 16-year time horizon. So let's look at 16 years instead.

  • If we look at the first 16 years of this time series (2005 through 2020), we have $2.5 million left, for a 4.7% return after distributions.
  • If we look at the latest 16 years of this time series (2009 through 2024), we have $3.8 million left, for a 9.0% return after distributions.

So using VDIGX, we can replicate Tyler's proposed scenario if we use the most recent 16 years, but not the 16 years ending in 2020, and not the 20 years ending in 2024. This observation calls for that old chestnut: "timing is everything." It may also be helpful to observe that with retrospective data snooping, you can achieve all kinds of fantastic results. Caveat emptor.

The 20-year return of FBGRX is attractive (in Scenarios A and B, above) but the risk is not as alluring. So let's explore one last question.

Alternatives: What's better than FBGRX?

Given the volatility of FBGRX, let's look at risk and return data from the past 20 years to see if we can find anything better than FBGRX, considering both risk and return. We'll search among five different types of portfolios:

  • Vanguard funds

  • iShares ETFs

  • Fidelity funds

  • Strategic (Static) DIY Portfolios

  • Tactical (Dynamic) DIY Portfolios

The FBGRX metrics over the past 20 years, which we aim to beat, are 12.9% annual return with 13.2% downside deviation.

After searching for a winner among the six different portfolio types, here's what we found:

  • Among Vanguard funds, nothing beats FBGRX in terms of both the risk and return. The Vanguard fund with a higher return and lowest risk was the Vanguard Dividend Growth with 10.5% annual return and 8.9% downside deviation.

  • Among iShares funds, nothing beats FBGRX in terms of both risk and return. The iShares ETF with a higher return and lowest risk was iShares Select Dividend ETF (DVY) with 7.9% annual return and 11.7% downside deviation.

  • Among other Fidelity funds, nothing beats FBGRX in terms of both risk and return. The Fidelity fund that came close, Fidelity Dividend Growth Fund (FDGFX), has an annual return of 8.4%, and 12.8% of downside deviation.

  • Among the tactical model portfolios that we track at RecipeInvesting.com, two did manage to have a higher return with lower risk.

    • Pure Momentum Portfolio (t.pure) with 15.3% annual return and 11.5% downside deviation

    • Adaptive Allocation Portfolio F (t.aaaf) with 14.1% annual return and 6.4% downside deviation.

Conclusion

Tyler's approach requires a portfolio with approximately 12% annualized return, which stems from needing 4% for withdrawals plus 8% for continued growth. Based on our research, a relatively high-risk fund is needed to make this work, and it works only for some time periods.

We looked at funds from Vanguard, Fidelity, and iShares, but did not find any funds with "dividend" in their name that could make Tyler's approach work over the past 20 years. Some tech funds that get close, but all of those come with higher risk. However, if you step into the world of tactical model portfolios or alpha-generating fund managers, you can find more choices for higher return portfolios with lower risk.

data.title: ECB Is Forced To Cut Rates Again This Week
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski

After the March meeting, the ECB seemed set for a pause at the next meeting. With interest rates at the upper end of the range for neutral interest rates estimates, taking a breather looked appropriate. Particularly as the europhoria after the German fiscal U-turn and strong European intentions to spend more on security and defence had clearly improved the eurozone’s growth outlook. However, since "Liberation Day", a pause is no longer an option.

US tariffs on the EU and many other countries have brought back growth concerns for the eurozone, at least in the nearer term. The strengthening of the euro as well as the drop in energy prices has added to the disinflationary forces the current trade tensions will have for the eurozone. As a result, the ECB, which looked hesitant to decide between a pause and a next rate cut only a few weeks ago, will have to continue its current easing cycle this week. As in October, a rate cut this week can easily be labelled as an insurance cut; a cut that can’t do any harm, while staying on hold would not only cast doubt about the ECB’s willingness to bolster growth but could also lead to a further and unwarranted strengthening of the euro. In case you missed it, the trade-weighted exchange rate of the euro is currently at the highest level since the start of the monetary union.

With the expected rate cut, the ECB will also have to change its communication. Instead of "monetary policy is becoming meaningfully less restrictive", the ECB is likely to flag that at 2.25%, the deposit rate would now be within the range of neutral interest rates.

Looking further ahead, there are at least two main challenges for the ECB. The ongoing trade tensions as well as a high level of uncertainty could force the ECB to cut interest rates further than it would currently like to admit. At the same time, the strengthening of the euro exchange rate, not only vis-à-vis the dollar, will insert more disinflationary pressure on the eurozone. Pre-empting some of this pressure could push the ECB to at least verbally open the door to accommodative monetary policy.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Vulcan Materials: A Good Buy At Current Levels
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Vulcan Materials Company's (NYSE:VMC) revenue should benefit from continued strength in public infrastructure markets, data centres, and heavy manufacturing. In addition, the warehousing market is also close to the bottom and seeing some green shoots. This should help the company more than offset some headwinds in residential and light non-residential due to the high interest rate environment. The pricing is also expected to be strong, with the company benefiting from the carry-over impact of mid-year price increases from last year, as well as price increases from earlier this year. In addition, the company has reasonable net leverage, and I am expecting bolt-on M&As to complement organic growth.

Margin should also benefit from price increases and cost optimization through the "Vulcan Way of Operating." The valuation is lower than historical, and I believe the stock is a good buy at current levels.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

After seeing good growth post-COVID, VMC's revenue growth was negatively impacted last year by elevated interest rates, which resulted in softer demand from the private construction sector, including residential and nonresidential (primarily warehouses) markets. However, a favorable pricing environment and continued growth in public construction activity (infrastructure markets) supported revenue growth last quarter.

In the fourth quarter of 2024, the company's revenues grew by 1.1% Y/Y to $1.853 billion. In the Aggregates segment, revenues increased by 4.2% Y/Y, driven by price increases, higher shipments for the public construction market, and strong demand in the storm-impacted areas of Western North Carolina and East Tennessee, partially offset by reduced private construction activity. Aggregates freight-adjusted selling prices were up 11% Y/Y, while the shipments decreased by 3% Y/Y. The Asphalt segment's revenues increased by 14.2% Y/Y, attributed to price increases and higher shipments. However, the Concrete segment's revenues were down 36.1% Y/Y due to tough Y/Y comps, as the prior year included revenues from the divested concrete operations in Texas (divested in November 2023).

Moving forward, the company is set to report its Q1 results on April 30th, and I am optimistic about the company's growth prospects. The company derives 40% of its revenue from public markets, which are seeing a good demand trend thanks to continued Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA) related spending. If we look at commentary by some of the other companies like United Rentals (URI) exposed to the infrastructure end market, out of more than $500 billion earmarked for infrastructure projects under IIJA, only $200 billion has been allocated, and $300 billion is still remaining to be assigned. Further, out of $200 billion allocated, only a fraction has been spent. So, there is a strong runaway for growth in the infrastructure market moving forward.

Additionally, $45 billion of funding was passed at the state and local level in the recent election cycle, which should help transportation infrastructure spending in the coming years. On its last earnings call, management noted that trailing 12 months highway starts increased by $7 billion to $122 billion, which should continue to drive aggregates demand for transportation projects in the coming years.

On the private market side, while there is some pressure due to the high interest rate environment, the robust Data Center activity and reshoring activity are helping the company offset it to a good extent. On the last earnings call, management cited co-star data, according to which, approximately 7% of proposed data center activity is within 20 miles of a Vulcan facility. This positions VMC well to capitalize on Data Center construction demand.

Management is also seeing some green shoots in the warehousing market and noted that warehouse starts have been flattening out (vs prior decline) on a trailing twelve-month basis and have turned positive on a trailing three-month basis, indicating we are close to the bottom and can see a potential rebound in the medium term. The warehouse market is a key driver for aggregate demand, and its bottoming out bodes well for the company from a medium to long-term perspective.

Residential and light non-residential markets continue to be weak due to the high interest rate environment. However, the interest rate cycle has started reversing, and I am expecting more cuts moving forward as the Federal Reserve takes steps to mitigate any potential slowdown caused by Trump Tariffs. This should drive recovery in these markets in the medium to long run.

Overall, I expect good public infrastructure demand, strength in data centers, and potential recovery in warehousing to more than offset near-term softness in the Residential and Light non-residential market, resulting in slightly positive organic aggregate volume.

The pricing environment also remains healthy. The company has posted good pricing increases over the last few years, and for the current year, management has guided for freight-adjusted aggregate pricing growth of between 5% and 7% despite a 100 bps negative mix impact from recent acquisitions.

VMC's CEO Tom Hill also indicated that the company will announce mid-year price increases, which are not included in the guidance given at the time of the last earnings call. So, that can result in upside to the pricing guidance for FY2025 as well, though most of the impact from mid-year increases should be felt in 2026. Below is the relevant excerpt:

... So they are not included in our guide, but we will absolutely announce midyear price increases. We will announce those probably towards the end of the first quarter, so we got time down those conversations."

- Tom Hill, Chairman and CEO, Vulcan Materials on Q42024 Earnings Call

So, flattish to slightly positive volume growth coupled with a healthy pricing increase in aggregates should help the company achieve good organic revenue growth.

In addition to organic growth, the company's FY25 revenues should also benefit from a full-year contribution from the 2024 acquisition. The company also had a reasonable net leverage of 2.3x at the end of the last year. So, there are also good chances of further bolt-on M&As in FY25.

Overall, I remain optimistic about the company's growth prospects in the near as well as long term.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

In Q4 2024, the company's margins benefited from price increases, operational efficiencies, and moderating inflationary input costs. As a result, the gross margin expanded by 330 bps Y/Y to 29%, and the adjusted EBITDA margin was up 370 bps Y/Y to 29.7%.

Segment-wise, the gross margin increased by 300 bps Y/Y in the Aggregates segment and 140 bps Y/Y in the Asphalt segment. In the Concrete segment, gross margin declined by 170 bps Y/Y, driven by the divestiture of the concrete operations in Texas, partially offset by price increases.

Moving forward, I am optimistic about the company's margin growth prospects. The company has seen moderation in inflationary cost pressures in the past few quarters. For FY25, management has guided for a 5% to 7% increase in freight-adjusted aggregate pricing and a low to mid single-digit increase in freight-adjusted aggregate unit cash cost. So, price/cost is expected to be positive moving forward. The company is not directly exposed to Trump Tariffs in a meaningful way. So, compared to other industries, it is better placed. The company is benefitting from the "Vulcan way of operating," where it is focusing on improving training, operator skills, equipment inspection, and leveraging technology for better throughput and reduced downtimes, which should continue to help it deliver incremental savings in the medium to long run. This, coupled with positive pricing, bodes well for a longer-term margin profile.

Valuation

VMC is trading at 28.88x FY25 consensus EPS estimates of $8.39 and 24.77X FY26 consensus EPS estimates of $9.78. This is a discount versus its 5-year historical average P/E (forward) of 31.79x.

While the company's forward P/E is higher than its peer Martin Marietta Materials (MLM), which is trading at 26.39x FY25 P/E and 22.46x FY26 P/E, its forward EV/EBITDA of 15.12x is almost in line with MLM's 15.54x.

I believe we are closer to the bottom in residential and light non-residential business, and as interest rates continue turning lower, these end markets should start recovering. This, coupled with strength in public infrastructure, heavy manufacturing (reshoring-related), and data centre end markets, and potential recovery in warehousing, bodes well for growth acceleration in the coming years. The company has already implemented healthy pricing increases in recent years. With the demand environment improving further in the medium term, pricing increases may further surprise on the upside. Given a good amount of pricing flows due to the bottom line, we should see further margin expansion and earnings growth acceleration.

Overall, VMC is a good long-term compounder to have in a portfolio. The stock has corrected in recent weeks despite good fundamentals and no meaningful direct exposure to tariffs, and is trading at a discount compared to historical levels. I believe management commentary at the time of the next earnings call should help ease some of the investor concerns, as the fundamental long-term growth story with pricing increases, margin improvement, and a solid demand environment in the public infrastructure space remains intact. Hence, I have a buy rating on the stock.

Risks

The company doesn't have direct exposure to tariffs. I am expecting a favourable tariff negotiation outcome, with most of the U.S. trading partners offering concessions in the form of lowered tariffs on the U.S. products. However, if these negotiations don't go as expected and result in stagflation in the U.S., the interest rate cycle reversal may be paused, and recovery in residential and light non-residential markets may not materialize. In this case, the growth acceleration I am expecting may not materialize.

In addition to organic growth, the company also pursues a bolt-on M&A strategy to drive growth. Inorganic growth is relatively risky, and there are risks related to overpaying for an acquisition or intended synergies not getting realized. If that happens, the stock price may be negatively impacted.

data.title: Microsoft: A Brilliant Business, But Here's Why It's Not In My Portfolio
data.description:
There is no doubt that Microsoft Corp. (NASDAQ:MSFT), along with OpenAI, are leading the way in the rise of AI. Yes, Nvidia (NVDA) is there as well, though they’re more in the business of selling the picks and shovels needed for AI. What I find more interesting is how AI is being used and what it’s actually helping to build. That’s where Microsoft comes in, with products like Azure OpenAI and Copilot.

The selloff in the broader markets has reshuffled the Mag 7 ranking by market cap, with Apple (AAPL) losing its leadership in the S&P 500 to Microsoft, (at the moment of writing this article) with a market cap of $2.90T and an enterprise value of $2.93T. Of course, seeing the volatility after yesterday's 10.5% jump, the #1 ranking will certainly fluctuate over the next weeks.

I admit in this introduction that analyzing Microsoft has been one of the most challenging tasks I encountered in the past years. Back in the old days (way before writing for SA), when I was a deep value investor, I wouldn't even touch anything with a mkt cap over $2 billion. Actually, that's usually where I set the upper limit on my screener. This being my initial coverage on Microsoft, I welcome constructive feedback on my thesis.

Overall, after reports came in pointing to a slowdown in Microsoft's data center buildout (although management recently reaffirmed the $80B CapEx), along with a more flexible (and, therefore, less favorable for Azure) partnership with Open AI, I don't see its premium valuation justified. So, let's start with dessert.

The Downside

Microsoft's overvaluation, when compared to its peers, was traditionally justified by its strong narrative. The company has strong ties with Open AI that trace back to 2019, which ultimately led to the Azure OpenAI service, the GitHub Copilot, and the Microsoft 365 Copilot.

This two-way partnership made perfect sense. Microsoft provides the infrastructure (data centers) to train Open AI's LLM and the tools for devs to build through Azure. OpenAI, on the other hand, brings the AI part (i.e., ChatGPT) to the devs' apps and websites.

Back in January, this partnership evolved to allow OpenAI to use cloud platforms beyond Microsoft's Azure. Initially, I wasn't concerned at all. In fact, it made sense, considering the $500 billion Stargate project. Why?

Because Open AI is starving for computing power. If they want to expand to GPT-5 and beyond, they need extra computing capacity. This means loosening some of the partnership rules with Microsoft so they can work with other data center and cloud providers.

Well, things stopped making sense after the TD Cowen report. The report was so influential that Microsoft had to step in and confirm they still plan to spend $80 billion on CapEx this year.

Isn't it interesting how, in almost any other company, investors generally want CapEx to do down in order to improve the free cash flow position of the company? Well, that is not the case for the big four hyperscalers. If their CapEx goes down, that's a signal that AI demand is not where everyone thought it would be.

According to Microsoft, their "plans to spend over $80B on infrastructure this FY remains on track as we continue to grow at a record pace to meet customer demand". However, Microsoft also said that it "may strategically pace or adjust our infrastructure in some areas."

Recently, reports came in stating that Microsoft will not be exercising a $12 billion option to buy additional data center capacity from CoreWeave. This, in addition to the slowdown of the $1 billion data center project in Ohio and others around the world, are not encouraging signs of demand for AI workloads.

Overall, I see a soft narrative that doesn't really justify its high valuation.

Valuation Comparison

The comparison analysis below includes Apple, Amazon (AMZN), Oracle (ORCL), and Google (GOOG). I believe these are the closest peers, even though their offerings might differ from Microsoft.

My discontent comes from the P/S ratio. Microsoft is trading over 3.5x Amazon on a P/S basis. That premium is often justified if the company is able to maximize sales into earnings (remember that NVIDIA is more expensive than AMD (AMD) on a P/S basis, but on a P/E basis, the difference is abysmal, 39 vs 96, respectively).

Therefore, seeing Microsoft's P/E in line with its peers is not particularly encouraging (at least, not to me). If the company traders at a premium on P/S, I want to see a discount on a P/E basis.

Even less encouraging is the P/CF ratio, which is the second highest in the group.

Overall, I can't say that Microsoft is a cheap company. That said, Microsoft has always been expensive, and the recent selloff has cooled off its valuation ratios, some of them now trading below their median.

The Upside

So far, Microsoft's AI business is showing strong signs of growth, with an annual revenue run rate in the past quarter of $13 billion, up 175% yoy.

Naturally, this led to the Azure and other cloud services revenue (see below) growth of 31% yoy in the past quarter.

According to the Q2 FY25 earnings call, a big part of Azure's growth came from AI services. Specifically, AI services made up 13 percentage points of the total 31 percent increase. However, I wouldn't pop a bottle of champagne just yet, as the growth seems to have slowed down since Q1 2024.

One metric that definitely is taking off is the commercial bookings growth. As seen below, the trend is quite encouraging, with a 67% yoy increase in the past quarter.

Furthermore, if we were to consider a constant currency basis (remember from the previous section that only half of Microsoft's revenue is generated in the US), the growth in the past quarter would have been 75%. Not bad. This ensures that revenue growth will continue its double-digit trajectory over the next few years. At the very least, until the partnership with Open AI comes to an end in 2030. Isn't it interesting that the Street's projections for revenue growth drop after 2030? (It's not a coincidence, in my view)

In fact, when asked during the Q&A, CFO Amy Hood said that most of the bookings growth was "driven by Azure commitments from OpenAI."

Looking at the financial statements, I have absolutely no complaints. The company looks healthy, with double-digit earnings and operating cash flow growth. Free cash flow is the only metric that is showing a clear deterioration, although, as I said before, this is positively welcomed by investors, as the increase in CapEx is a direct sign that demand for AI is solid. Speaking of CapEx, the estimate for this year is $80 billion, below Amazon's $100 billion but above Alphabet's $75 billion and Meta's $60 - $65 billion.

Other than this, Microsoft is seeing strong demand for Copilot. This is reflected in the 16% yoy increase in Microsoft 365's commercial revenue. Additionally, the number of consumer subscribers is still increasing, which is a reflection of how sticky Microsoft 365 is once you have adopted the ecosystem.

Conclusion

To wrap up, I have a high conviction that Microsoft remains a strong company at the heart of the AI expansion cycle. In the past quarter, Azure revenue increased by 31% yoy, mainly driven by AI services, contributing 13 points. That said, at a P/S ratio over 3.5 times Amazon and a P/CF second highest among its peers, the valuation leaves little room for error.

On the positive side, the company has reaffirmed its CapEx guidance of $80 billion this year. However, recent pullbacks, like walking away from a $12 billion CoreWeave option or slowing down the Ohio $1B data center project, raise questions as to how high the demand for AI workload is.

Unless earnings accelerate meaningfully, it’s hard to justify paying 34x forward earnings. At $2.9 trillion in market cap, the upside looks quite limited to me.

data.title: Modiv Industrial Sits In The Crosshairs Of Tariffs
data.description:
The past several months have set the stage to materially change trading norms around the globe. As tariffs take hold, the economy is trying to make heads and tails of a new reality. Sitting center stage in these changes in Modiv Industrial (NYSE:MDV), a net lease REIT that acts as a sale leaseback partner for operators of industrial real estate.

In November, I initiated coverage on MDV providing an overview of the company’s portfolio, business model, and short operating history. The article was cautionary, focusing on the risks of sale leaseback investing.

The purpose of today’s article is to follow up, review the recent decline since our Sell rating, and discuss a deteriorating outlook amidst speed bumps to domestic investment and a looming trade conflict. Without further ado…

Who Is Modiv Industrial?

In my prior coverage of MDV, I provided an update on the company’s portfolio, including the asset count and annualized base rent.

MDV’s portfolio consists of 43 properties generating an annualized base rent of $40.2 million. The properties cover 4.5 million square feet, meaning each property averages around 100,000 square feet.

MDV owns 39 industrial assets that account for their core portfolio. These assets account for 77% of ABR. The remaining portion are generated from non-core assets, which includes a KIA auto dealership in Los Angeles and several other non-industrial assets that the company is actively working on turning over.

Based on the most recent filings from the company, the portfolio has not changed significantly. As of the end of 2024, MDV owned 43 operating properties, comprised of 39 industrial properties and four noncore assets. Looking deeper, the largest industry is infrastructure, with 17 properties accounting for nearly 25% of annualized base rent and 28% of portfolio square footage. The next two largest categories are automotive and industrial products, accounting for 15% and 14% of annualized based rent, respectively.

The company's largest individual geography is California, accounting for nearly $12 million in annualized base rent, or 30% at the portfolio level.

Review of Prior Coverage

In November, I initiated coverage on MDV. The purpose of the article was to introduce the business, discuss the company’s strategy, and outline key risks that I felt the market was possibly overlooking in the valuation of MDV. While I clearly did not believe that MDV was headed for bankruptcy with any imminence, I rated the company a Sell.

A large piece of my prior coverage centered around MDV’s sale leaseback heavy business model. Like many net lease REITs, MDV acts as a capital partner for private equity sponsored businesses. They act as a piggy bank to buy the real estate from companies that are either growing, or more likely the case for industrial companies, recapitalizing. These aging businesses are often conservatively capitalized and generationally owned, giving PE an opportunity to strip away the assets, lever up, and provide healthy returns to their shareholders all while reducing their risk.

Part of my thesis is below:

MDV needs time to prove itself, mature their business, and establish a portfolio worth buying…. However, I would not have an interest in being a shareholder given the inherent risk embedded in their business. MDV earns a Sell rating.

The publication ruffled more feathers than expected. From my perspective, MDV was facing risks associated with tenant creditworthiness and exposure to economic changes from the incoming administration. In the meantime, MDV continued to climb and outperform the net lease sector.

Since publication, shares of MDV have fallen by 13.3%. Although the shares have declined with a degree of consistency since November, the announcement of recent tariffs have been the nail in the coffin for the already volatile market. The recent decline has changed MDV’s pricing, pushing the yield to over 8%. From one perspective, this presents a different opportunity set than just five or six months ago, with higher return through the dividend and greater potential for the company’s multiple to expand. However, I am not ready to accept such an optimistic view.

Once again, I believe the market is significantly underestimating the potential impact of President Trump’s economic policies to certain industrial REITs.

Economic Policy Could Hurt MDV

One thing is certain, times are changing quickly. President Trump has unleashed an arsenal of what I have been calling “novel” policies. This means policies and strategies with little historical precedence, which inherently increases uncertainty around the impacts of those policies. The tariff plan would qualify as one such policy that will certainly impact American business. Let’s explore the implication of some of these changes for MDV.

Over the past year, President Trump has expressed interest in either revoking or overhauling Biden’s CHIPS act. CHIPS stands for Creating Helpful Incentives to Produce Semiconductors.

What is CHIPS?

The CHIPS act was an economic initiative designed to accelerate investment in the United States semiconductor manufacturing. The incentive program was designed to encourage foreign investment in the United States and enhance our production capacity.

The Peterson Institute provides an overview of the act and its short five-year history.

While the US dominates much of the chip supply chain, by 2020 it held a small and shrinking share of semiconductor fabrication, with no production of advanced chips on American soil. US policymakers believed that attracting production to US territory would reduce the risk that virtually all the world’s advanced semiconductor manufacturing could be shut off in an invasion or blockade of Taiwan and would benefit US national security and hopefully lead to positive spillovers for the US economy and technological innovation. The CHIPS Act induced many of the world’s leading semiconductor companies to produce chips in the US by offering a generous advanced manufacturing tax credit of 25 percent to facilities that make semiconductors or equipment that makes chips

The primary takeaway from the short history of CHIPS is that it has been wildly successful. Over just several years, construction of electronics production facilities in the United States has increased by approximately tenfold, much of which can be linked to CHIPS.

The CHIPS Act has been largely hailed as a win. Demand for U.S. manufacturing space soared as the first round gave $39B in loans, and $220B in development was announced in its first year alone.

Despite the success of the CHIPS act, it appears as though the current administration is considering modifying scrapping the program entirely.

Trump May End CHIPS

Over the past year, President Trump has expressed interest in ending CHIPS in favor of tariffs.

President Donald Trump has pulled execution of the CHIPS Act under a new entity overseen by Secretary of Commerce Howard Lutnick, who reportedly intends to take a tougher position on the program meant to spur domestic semiconductor manufacturing.

Rather than incentivizing trade partners to invest inside of the United States, tariffs would punish American businesses for operating/investing outside of the United States for the supply chains. One could argue that these are two very different roads to arrive at the same location. Putting that aside, revocation of the act would be a considerable disruption for the ongoing wave of manufacturing facilities.

Ending the bill would not only disrupt the ongoing wave of construction but would likely reduce the effectiveness of any future government programs as trust is broken between administration changes. Instead, the current administration may be better off by devising legislation that would promote investment in targeted sectors of manufacturing, such as semiconductors. CHIPS was effective in bringing foreign investment to the United States, a significant economic driver. Accounting for the past five years of inflation, the act spurred over $100 billion in investment inside of the United States, more than the entire amount of manufacturing capacity produced in the United States over the previous decade.

Unlike tariffs, which are likely to have an inflationary impact on the price of goods, the CHIPS act was able to spur investment without raising costs directly while also increasing our competition with China. If the government eliminates incentive programs for domestic investment, foreign producers could see significantly reduced motivation to build inside of America.

Conclusion

As it relates to MDV, the business model of the company is heavily dependent on the growth trajectory of manufacturing businesses inside of the United States. Going further, irrespective of growth, the company depends on the manufacturing industry, which has been struggling to revitalize over the past decade. As the government unveils strong initiatives which are designed to foster manufacturing inside of America, disruption is almost certain to have an overall negative impact. For a capital partner like MDV, these disruptions can be significant enough to tenants to cause material issues which lead to rent relief or, in a worst-case scenario, bankruptcy.

The specialization of many facilities owned by MDV dictates that the company would likely have a difficult time releasing facilities as is. A more likely scenario would be an expensive scrape and redevelopment, which is costly and disadvantageous during a tumultuous market. With these risks in mind, the increasing risk factors associated with an investment in MDV are simply too great for the company to look attractive. While the recent declines in share price have made the company more attractive, the macroeconomic disruption and perky outlook for US manufacturing earns the company a Hold rating.

data.title: Playstudios: Few Positives Aside From Its Cheap Valuation
data.description:
Investment thesis

While Q4 results for Playstudios (NASDAQ:MYPS) were within management's guidance, the company's outlook for 2025 pointed to an expectation for lower revenue and adjusted EBITDA. While the stock is cheaply valued, especially with its net cash position accounting for 60% of the current market cap, when taking a look at key metrics related to its rewards platform, I am concerned with some fundamental issues I see. While I bring up certain positive aspects and potential upside catalysts, I am inclined to stick to the sidelines for now.

Low valuation reflects underlying risks and concerns

Based on the midpoint of management's FY25 adjusted EBITDA guidance of $50 million, shares are valued at an EV to adjusted EBITDA multiple of just 1.4, when accounting for the company's net cash position of $109 million. Notably, its net cash position represents 60% of its current market cap. This valuation multiple represents a large discount compared to a relevant peer like Playtika (PLTK) which has also struggled to generate organic growth and is valued at a mid-single digit adjusted EBITDA multiple.

While I recognize that further downside could be limited by the large net cash position and the business being consistently FCF positive, it is difficult to foresee major upside with the current guidance implying that this year would continue a similar trend since 2023 where both revenue and adjusted EBITDA have seen considerable year-over-year declines. Furthermore, since stock-based compensation, licensing costs and capex together represent more than $40 million annually, which are not included in the adjusted EBITDA figure, it is noteworthy that GAAP earnings are substantially lower.

I would also point out that while the company's management team has on several occasions remarked that its stock is grossly undervalued, including during the Q4 earnings call, there has been only an insignificant amount of capital allocated towards share repurchases in the last two quarters.

Besides the company's declining revenue and adjusted EBITDA figures, I would point to some fundamental issues associated with its Playawards platform, with the retail value of purchases being down 38% year over year in Q4, as shown above. Other metrics like Average Daily Active Users ("DAU") and Average Monthly Active Users ("MAU") also saw year-over-year declines of 19% and 14% respectively. While its CEO mentioned that this was due to an ongoing cleaning up of some of its platform mechanics, it remains unclear as to when this process will be completed and if the deterioration in these key metrics will continue going forward. These aspects definitely shake my confidence in the business, and its ability to drive user engagement and retention on its platform, especially as the company also no longer expects to be able to launch its Loyalty as a Service offering, which was previously touted as an opportunity to be a high margin revenue source.

Turning to other risks, the recent deterioration in the macroeconomy which has led to weaker consumer sentiment will also be an additional headwind that the company has to deal with.

Positive aspects and potential catalysts

Besides its low valuation, there are some positives which I believe could drive upside for investors. One such factor is the increasing number of in-app purchases conducted through its direct-to-consumer channel. This accounted for 8% of in-app purchase revenue in Q4, and it comes at significantly higher gross margins as it avoids app store fees. Here, based on what I've seen with other mobile gaming companies, I agree with management that this number could exceed 20% of in-app purchase revenue in the coming years.

While management also carried out a restructuring program in Q4 with the aim of reducing operating expenses, a major portion of the savings are planned for the development and marketing on its upcoming sweepstakes initiative, set to launch later this year. This is a key catalyst I see for the stock. The sweepstakes market has grown rapidly in recent years and the management estimates that the sweepstakes market is worth around $4.5 billion, something that is further validated by other market participants. While sweepstakes was not mentioned by management until the second half of last year, I would point out that a reference to it was made more than forty times during the last earnings call, thus highlighting its significance. Notably, management has not included any contribution from this initiative in current guidance, though they did mention that a successful rollout could lead to between $15 million to $30 million in revenue upside this year.

Lastly, given the company's large cash position and capacity to take on debt if needed, I do acknowledge that a transformative acquisition could be a potential upside catalyst that investors can look for.

Conclusion

Although the valuation of MYPS stock is extremely cheap, offering limited downside, I have walked through several risks and concerns related to the business and its outlook. While I have highlighted some positives and potential catalysts that could offer investors meaningful upside, it is too early for me to judge the likelihood of success and this should instead be monitored in the coming quarters. For now, I recommend staying away from the stock and therefore maintain my Hold rating.

data.title: PBDC: Valuation Reversion Makes BDCs A Sector Buy
data.description:
Since the beginning of the year, business development companies have found themselves at the heart of investor attention as volatility, changing interest rate expectations, and increasing uncertainty continue to shape the market. The President’s novel economic policies have caused increasing uncertainty across every corner of the global economy, and the markets are reeling.

BDCs, which primarily invest in middle market private companies, offer a blend of exposure to private credit and private equity. For most investors, these assets offer yield and diversification. Over the past several years, the rise of the asset class has been exceptional. However, as the Federal Reserve’s monetary policy now shows signs of change and the global economy stutters, BDC valuations have started to show signs of mean reversion that warrant attention.

Today’s publication from REITer’s Digest explores the current state of the business development company market, with an emphasis on valuation dynamics and mean reversion. Over the past several months, I have published several critical articles covering business development companies. This article will follow up on the thesis, which predicted a significant shift in valuations for some of the sector’s largest participants. As the sector shifts, we will explore the sector in its entirety via the Putnam BDC Income ETF (NYSEARCA:PBDC), the only actively managed exchange-traded fund of BDCs.

Fund Overview

In February, I initiated coverage on PBDC, one of the largest exchange-traded funds covering the sector. In fact, PBDC is one of the only funds covering BDCs, which are still considered a risky and niche asset class by many investors. However, I believe the long-term performance of some of the sector’s all stars warrant their consideration. For example, Gladstone Investment (GAIN), a smaller BDC and my personal favorite, has trounced the S&P 500 since the financial crisis by blending debt and equity investing.

My prior coverage of PBDC included a comprehensive overview of the fund's construction and active management process from the manager, Putnam. Included in the previous coverage was an analysis of the fund's assets under management period. PBDC still manages just $175,000,000 in assets, making the fund a smaller ETF.

Also clarified was an explanation of the fund's complicated expense ratio. Despite fund level expenses looking exorbitantly high as quoted on the top line, we uncover through the fund's annual report that the ETF charges just 75 basis points as a management fee and the remainder corresponds to fees charged by the fund's individual holdings.

Another primary consideration with funds like PBDC is the concentration risk associated with the business development company sector. The business development company sector has relatively few participants. Accordingly, the fund has only 24 holdings and the top five account for nearly 40% of the fund's total assets. The top 10 holdings of the fund account for more than 70% of assets.

That said, each individual business development company is thoroughly diversified, usually holding hundreds if not thousands of individual loans from a wide variety of different borrowers. Typically, in the world of business development companies and private credit, diversification is the name of the game and managers are best suited for spreading their risk as widely as possible across the economy.

The performance of the fund in the sector has also been strong. Despite recent losses, performance of the fund since inception remains positive and performance of some of the largest participants in the sector continues to power forward during the higher for longer era.

However, the past several weeks have put forth a serious speed bump for business development company investors. Participants across the market have encountered the same volatility which has struck some of the largest companies around the globe. Over a period of weeks, the business development company sector has fallen in line with the broader market, beginning to decline precipitously.

Let’s explore BDC valuations in more detail and explain the current change in pricing.

Valuation Reversion Strikes Again

Over the past six months, I have written several cautionary articles covering the BDC sector. Although I have rated the sector a buy throughout the rising interest rates and higher for longer eras, pricing has recently begun to deteriorate. The market turmoil has left no stone unturned, and the tide has gone out on nearly every asset class. This includes BDCs, despite being a resilient asset class. In fact, several of these articles that were published over the past two months are relevant as some of PBDC’s largest individual holdings.

At the beginning of February, I published an article covering Ares Capital (ARCC), rating the fund a Hold. Despite being a top performing BDC, ARCC was priced at nearly 1.2x book value and looked historically expensive on the basis of dividend and earnings yields. My thesis was simple:

To put it succinctly, ARCC has little room for upside, trading at a compressed yield and expanded price to book value.

Since publication, ARCC shares have declined by nearly 13% as the tide goes out on one of the market’s largest business development companies.

In the meantime, the company’s price to book ratio has now declined to 0.98x on a forward basis. With the fund’s valuation zoomed out, we’ll note that today’s price looks more aligned with the market’s historical valuation of the company.

Perhaps an even better example is Main Street Capital (MAIN). In February, I published a controversial article covering MAIN and rating the company a Sell.

The article was controversial, generating nearly 130 comments, many of whom were dissenters who disagreed with the rating. Although I was a long term MAIN investor, share prices had reached what I believe were an unreasonable valuation. In the article, I provided an explanation of my decision to sell my shares.

While MAIN continues to price at twice net asset value, both alternatives offer attractive risk-adjusted yields relative to MAIN. Should MAIN return to the midpoint valuation, an investment may be warranted. However, while MAIN’s valuation continues to sit well above historical averages and, from my perspective, logic. Taking profits looks to be a reasonable option. Especially when a yield within 100bps can be obtained via a money market fund with minimal risks and dollar in, dollar out pricing.

At publication, MAIN was trading at a price to net asset value of 2.0x, significantly above the company’s historical valuation. On one hand, MAIN’s equity holdings continue to perform well and likely imply some unincorporated gains that could make NAV slightly soft. Even still, pricing at double book value was too rich for a business development company like MAIN.

Since publication, shares of MAIN have declined by more than 15%, now sitting around $50 per share. As MAIN once again begins to stabilize, investors may note that the company’s forward price to book value is around 1.6x, in line with the company’s five-year rolling average according to Seeking Alpha.

On a combined basis, these two companies account for more than 15% of PBDC’s total holdings. Over the past several months, both funds have undergone a significant valuation reversion. Shares have fallen significantly year to date, reaching their historical valuation midpoints on a net asset value basis. From this perspective, BDCs look considerably more attractive than just six months ago.

Once again, there is a lesson to be learned that trees do not often grow to the sky. History has the potential to act as an anchor point for investor psychology.

Conclusion

From my perspective, it is this change in valuation at the market level which has begun to impact the pricing of business development companies. For a diversified fund which covers a material portion of the market’s participants, a significant decrease in share price is almost always caused by systemic risk, rather than individual impact. Systemic risk is certainly rising with the specter of a trade war growing and unrelenting turmoil in the markets. That said, the business development company sector is simply returning to a more reasonable point. PBDC is a relatively new fund with only several years of operating history.

Zooming out, we will quickly notice that PBDC has still been profitable for investors since the fund’s launch in 2022. Total return remains positive despite the recent declines. With interest rates remaining higher for longer and valuations looking attractive, PBDC earns a Buy rating.
